data.title: Brinker International: A Good Time To EAT
data.description:
After a blockbuster year, casual dining chain Brinker International (EAT) is poised for a defining 2025. The tussle between macro headwinds pressurizing consumer discretionary spending and a continued growth led by operational efficiencies and menu optimizations is going to define the road ahead.

Traffic Recovery Against Macro Sensitivity

After facing pressures from rising costs that eroded margins and seeing lower traffic post COVID, Brinker started turning around its margin recovery efforts visibly from late 2023. A 21% traffic increase has been reported in Q3 2025 recently (for quarter ending March 2025). The turnaround strategy has been based on the executional pillars typical of casual dining chains - operational efficiencies, value positioning and menu optimizations. Clearly the strategy is working so far with traffic increasing and same store sales showing visible upticks. This also shows increasing customer engagement and stickiness (over only expansion-driven growth), primarily led by the flagship Chili's brand.

The same store sales jump has been in an environment of significant topline growth, which makes it an even stronger turnaround. The margins are also improving while the same store sales have gone up, even at the operating level. This points to greater sustainability over strategies like short-term campaigns leading to temporary upticks.

The overall traction backed by margin expansion is driven by an optimal pricing approach which has helped Brinker absorb the food cost increases due to inflation and menu mix changes. Pricing is often the holy grail in casual dining. As of now, it can be said that Brinker has been able to establish the optimal prices that are drawing in customers without impacting its financials.

Brinker has also been able to scale its revenue without a disproportionate labor cost increase, pointing to operational efficiencies. On the customer-facing side, partnership with Red Hat to drive serving robots in some places and overall unification of digital platforms to simplify customer engagement and experience, shows Brinker's commitment to tech-based efficiencies.

The story of growth at controlled expenses and enhanced customer stickiness has all the boxes ticked so far. But what about the macro headwinds? Management remains fairly confident and have revised their estimates upwards for fiscal 2025. That is great, but that covers the next quarter only. Fresh management updates in the FY 2025 Earnings Session will be key to get a guidance for the upcoming fiscal year.

I see pressures emerging on EAT due to a drop in discretionary spending. And also from the taller comparable growth ask in same stores sales after a stellar run. The current management's ability to adapt to changing environments is established given the turnaround in the past couple of years (growing stickiness at stable margins is a hug tick in an industry where moat is difficult). EAT is positioned to respond well given the cost efficiencies and customer stickiness they have built now.

However, there will be pressure on margins and topline, even if at reduced levels, before any recovery. Brinker's brand stickiness and price elasticity is expected to place them better in a lower customer spend regime than a fine dining positioning.

And How is EAT Valued

Undervalued in terms of forward EV to EBITDA of peers, but slightly above its own historical averages.

There are some concerns around the higher level of debt for EAT compared to the peers who are valued higher. However, I see some breakaway improvements in both Free Cash and EBITDA that should allay some of the debt concerns. Improving margins, as observed earlier will surely reduce the gap here.

A significant portion of the undervaluation is also because of how the peers have consistently grown and performed compared to EAT's only recent turnaround. From that perspective, the information arbitrage we have is the green shoots' strength we have seen in the turnaround so far. Markets may be waiting for sustained growth to reflect in multiple expansion, but that could be too late to invest, particularly if you believe in the early green shoots. This could actually be a good zone to time a buy.

What Should Investors Do

Summarizing the positives

  • Two quarters of same store sales increase is a confirmation of the turnaround in traffic and customer stickiness
  • Topline and margins both have been growing during this turnaround
  • Focus on cost efficiencies and pricing seems to have gone right and places EAT well for any downturn
  • Overall turnaround gives confidence in management's ability to adapt to a downturn
  • Valuation multiples seem to have not reacted to the green shoots yet. Could be an ideal time to invest before growth is factored in.
  • Free cash and margin improvements will help allay some of the debt concerns

In terms of drags

  • The debt load is higher than peers. Improving scene.
  • Sustained growth is yet to be seen. Does not impact investment thesis.
  • Valuation expensive compared to own history. But moderate overvaluation and attributable to the turnaround. In fact, could be the sweet spot to invest.
  • Macro headwinds. Although better placed than fine dine and shown customer loyalty.
  • Tougher comps for next earnings cycles after blockbuster turnaround
  • Mostly reliant on single brand Chili and not aggressively expanding. The health of the growth is more important.

Overall, it is good to note the rally in EAT is not a significant valuation expansion, but more a reflection of the turnaround in earnings. That gives an opportunity to initiate "Buy" at these levels too. Expect some stagnation or volatility if the macro headwinds are tougher, but the overall positives outweigh the negatives. It is a good time to EAT while valuations haven't reacted much to the strong turnaround signs - waiting for sustained growth and in the face of macro headwinds.

data.title: NKX: Despite The Recent Drop, I Still Can't See A Buy Argument
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the Nuveen California AMT-Free Quality Municipal Income Fund (NKX) as an investment option at the current market price. This is a closed-end fund [CEF] that offers investors direct exposure to the muni market within the state of California. It has an objective to "provide current income exempt from regular federal income tax and the alternative minimum tax applicable to individuals and California income tax and to enhance portfolio value".

My followers know I cover the muni sector regularly and NKX hit my radar when the year got started. However, I was reluctant to choose this option for California muni exposure and my caution appears to be have been vindicated since I wrote that review:

Clearly it made sense to be cautious on NKX - along with most of the market for that matter! But with muni prices declining and investors continuing to look for value outside large-cap US equities, I figured it was time to take another look at this CEF to see if I should upgrade my rating.

After review, I still am not convinced this is the best option for muni investors. The fund is not cheap, and its distribution level is a bit of a mirage. For these reasons, and others, I will be keeping the "hold" outlook in place and I will use this review to justify that decision.

Valuation Story Has Gotten Worse

One of my primary concerns for NKX has to do with valuation. On the surface, the fund doesn't seem wildly expensive - and it isn't. But it still has a premium near 2% and that is something that would make me somewhat cautious as a general rule:

In fairness, this isn't a reason to avoid a fund in isolation, especially if one is bullish about the underlying securities within it. But my problem, is there is a general weakness within NKX's portfolio. I know this because when I covered this fund back in February it actually sat with a slight discount to NAV. And beyond just getting more expensive, this is during a stretch of time that NKX lost value - as the opening paragraph showed.

The problem here is two-fold. NKX is seeing its share price decline while simultaneously getting more expensive. That is not a great backdrop and I would struggle to come up with a positive spin on that development. With other muni CEFs - including ones focused on debt from California - offering a better value proposition, this wholly supports my decision to hold off on upgrading my rating on this particular fund.

The Yield Story Is Still Mixed

The second topic is a repeat from my January article, so I won't dive into too much detail here to avoid rehashing the same issue. But I do want to make a point to at least mention it - and this concerns the fund's income stream. On the surface, this looks to be a strong attribute, as NKX is above the 7.5% mark:

After taxes are considered - especially for those in the top state and federal brackets, this yield reaches double digits. This begs the question - what's not to like?

Well, consistent with my last review, the nuts and bolts of it is that this distribution level is really not sustainable long-term. So while we can collect generous tax-exempt income now, the eventual reduction of this monthly distribution payout is going to cause some volatility in the future.

I would remind readers that this distribution is heavily reliant on return of capital to be paid at this level. The positive takeaway is that this metric has improved since my last review when it sat at 50%. But that improvement is mild when we consider where it sits today:

The bottom-line is NKX's yield is going to have to be reduced eventually. This is a point I made earlier this year and, clearly, it hasn't happened yet. But this level of return of capital is going to weigh on the fund over time, so either the NAV is going to lose value consistent or the distribution will become more in-line with actual net investment income. Either way, this is a big headwind investors will need to contend with and keeps me from recommending the fund.

I Continue To Like Municipal Bonds Holistically

I laid out a couple of reasons why I am not keen on the idea of buying NKX. But I want to reiterate that I am bullish on munis overall and that is why I don't see merit to downgrading my view below "hold" for this CEF. The fact is, I believe munis offer a strong value proposition in this macro-environment and that is likely to remain the case throughout 2025. So, if the risk profile for NKX changes, so too might my rating.

Supporting this outlook I have is the fact that the trade uncertainty is nowhere near "over". While markets have rebounded swiftly and the VIX's decline shows investors are no longer fearing the worst, we have to remember that tariffs are still likely to go in place at some level. And their impact is far-reaching and significant across a number of key trading partners for the US:

What I am driving at here is there is plenty of macro/geopolitical risk out there in the market today. Does that mean we are due for another equity tank? Maybe not. But it does suggest to me that de-risking some aspects of my portfolio makes a lot of sense here. Munis are one of my preferred methods to make such a move given their historical stability and desirable income streams. Thus, I am reiterating that I remain a bull of this sector, just not the particular fund isolated in this review.

This Fund Is Well Diversified Within California

Looking into NKX specifically, there are some positive attributes about the fund as I see them. One is that while this CEF is exclusive to California issues - and therefore concentrated in that regard - it is diversified quite well among the different sub-sectors within the state's muni issuance:

I think this offers a nice spread between General Obligation [GO] muni bonds - which are generally considered the safest type - and revenue bonds. Further, I would note this is especially relevant for the state of California in that the state's finances are concentrated in a unique way. While most states have some form of progressive taxation, levels vary widely. Some jurisdictions don't have any state income taxes while others, such as California, have relatively high rates on the top earners. This dynamic leaves the Golden State especially reliant on high-income earners paying a disproportionate amount of receipts:

What I am driving at here is that investors in a state-specific muni fund already face some level of concentration risk. Then, in the case of California, this is a state heavily reliant on a strong stock market and overall economy because the top earners - who perform well in those scenarios - are paying the bulk of states of the state. So investors in muni securities originating from California need the broader country to be doing well, despite owning "California specific" bonds.

To tie this back to how I opened this discussion, this is central to why I see NKX's sector make-up as an important attribute for the fund. This sector diversity helps to balance out the state and wealth concentration risks that are inherent in this type of investment strategy. NKX does a good job mitigating this impact through its diverse holdings and that is central to why I will keep this CEF on my radar going forward.

Health Care Sector Reliant On Federal Funds

My final point is another word of caution. It is not meant to be alarmist, because overall, I believe the state of California is going to make good on its muni debt obligations and I don't see a likely scenario where federal funding is slashed dramatically to the state. That is my thesis.

But it is worth pointing out that the Trump administration and a Republican-led Congress are not exactly "California friendly". There are concerns that budgets cuts could come about, especially as the federal government continues to come into conflict with state and local governments over social issues. This is relevant to California because the state relies heavily on federal dollars to provide health services to its public - especially those in low-income households. In fact, if we look at the proposed budget for the upcoming fiscal year, we can see quite distinctly how health-related expenditures capture the federal funds that flow into the state:

I'm emphasizing this topic because NKX is heavily invested in health care munis. I shared the graphic earlier in this review that shows this sector clocks in over 22% of total fund assets. So this is an investment that would face a serious headwind if federal funding does get cut going forward. Again, I'm not predicting that it will, but this is definitely a risk to consider and NKX faces an elevated risk in that regard.

Bottom-Line

NKX has been struggling of late, and I don't see that changing. The fund's valuation has gone from slight discount to premium, and I still see a distribution that is not well-supported through net investment income. While I like the underlying diversification and see continued demand from high-income earners for tax-exempt debt, I believe there are better options out there for retail investors. I am therefore keeping my "hold" rating in place and urge my followers to be very selective on any entry points at this time.

data.title: An Integrated Public/Private Portfolio Analytics Framework
data.description:
[The adoption of alternative investment options inside investment portfolios has been driven by greater accessibility and the search for higher returns, better diversification, and enhanced risk mitigation. Additionally, the growing sophistication of investors and their desire for personalized strategies are pushing advisors to explore alternative options.

While this trend is geared to ultimately benefit the client, it is putting stress on the way home office investment teams are able to construct, manage, and rebalance these expanded and complex positions in investor portfolios. In fact, most firms outside the largest global wealth institutions do not have a sound analytical framework to manage the nature of this demand at scale. The framework needed would have to enable an integrated analysis of public and private assets in portfolio construction, offering wealth managers a systematic way to analyze, integrate, and optimize portfolios, including significant private holdings.

To understand the changing portfolio management landscape and how wealth management firms are responding, we reached out to Alex Kokolis, Managing Director, Head of the Wealth Management Segment at MSCI Wealth. Our conversation explored these prevailing wealth management trends and the discussion was particularly timely as MSCI had just published new research on Total Portfolio Allocation for Modern Wealth Management highlighting the benefits of better diversification and personalization of investor portfolios.]

Hortz: Can you share your perspectives on the rise of alternative investment options in wealth management?

Alex Kokolis: The wealth management landscape has evolved significantly over the past few years. Alternative investments, including private equity, private credit, real estate, and infrastructure funds have become increasingly popular among wealth managers and their clients. We have seen a seismic shift in the accessibility of these assets through new delivery vehicles such as interval and evergreen funds, as well as in the demand by investors in search for higher returns, diversification, and the desire to mitigate risks associated with traditional asset classes like stocks and bonds.

Hortz: What are some of the key issues driving advisory firms to allocate more of their client portfolios into private capital funds?

Alex Kokolis: Several factors are contributing to this trend.

Firstly, the prolonged low-interest-rate environment has made traditional fixed-income investments less attractive. Advisors are looking for ways to enhance returns, and private capital funds offer the potential for higher yields.

Secondly, the increased volatility in public markets has led advisors to seek more stable and predictable investment options. Private capital funds often have longer investment horizons and can provide a buffer against market fluctuations.

Lastly, the growing sophistication of investors and their desire for personalized investment strategies have pushed advisors to explore alternative assets that align with their clients' unique goals and risk tolerance.

Hortz: As these portfolio allocations shift, what challenges do home office investment teams face in wealth management firms?

Alex Kokolis: One of the primary challenges is the need for a new analytical framework that allows investment teams to analyze and rebalance portfolios across both public and private funds simultaneously. Traditional portfolio management tools are often designed for public market investments and may not adequately capture the complexities of private capital funds. Investment teams must develop robust methodologies to assess the performance, risk, and liquidity of these alternative assets.

Then they need to develop an integrated approach to also ensure that diversification is not just about adding more markets but about helping to manage risk more effectively through a deeper understanding of market correlations.

Additionally, they need to ensure that the integration of private capital funds into investor portfolios aligns with the overall investment strategies and objectives.

Hortz: Can you elaborate on the analytical framework that wealth management firms need to adopt for this purpose?

Alex Kokolis: The analytical framework should encompass several key components.

Firstly, it must include comprehensive data collection and analysis capabilities for private capital funds. This involves gathering detailed information on fund performance, fees, liquidity, and risk factors.

Secondly, the framework should incorporate advanced modeling techniques to simulate various scenarios and assess the impact of private capital funds on the overall portfolio.

Thirdly, it should enable dynamic rebalancing, allowing investment teams to adjust allocations in response to changing market conditions and client needs.

Finally, the framework should facilitate transparent reporting and communication with clients, ensuring they understand the rationale behind all investment decisions and the benefits of including alternative assets in their portfolios.

Hortz: What has your research shown you as to how best to address these demands?

Alex Kokolis: We have taken the approach of using our factor models as a unifying method of analysis. Our research report, Total Portfolio Allocation for Modern Wealth, has shown that multi-asset-class factor models can provide a sophisticated framework for integrating private and public assets into a coherent portfolio strategy by analyzing assets based on underlying performance drivers, such as equity, interest rates, inflation, country, and industry, rather than traditional asset class analysis. This approach helps understand the fundamental drivers of portfolio risk and return, especially for private assets where historical return-based correlation estimates may not be reliable.

These factor models are central to this framework, offering a unified method for analyzing diverse investments and estimating correlations between markets to create personalized portfolio solutions.

Hortz: How do you see the future of alternative investments in wealth management evolving?

Alex Kokolis: The future of alternative investments in wealth management can be an important building block of portfolios going forward. As investors continue to seek higher returns and enhanced diversification, the demand for private capital funds is likely to continue to grow. Technological advances will play a crucial role in facilitating the integration of these assets into investor portfolios. Enhanced data analytics, artificial intelligence, and machine learning will enable investment teams to make more informed decisions and optimize portfolio performance.

Additionally, regulatory developments may provide greater transparency and accessibility to alternative investments, making them more attractive to a broader range of investors. Overall, I believe that alternative investments will become commonplace as part of most wealth management strategies.

Hortz: What variables should investors and their financial advisors consider when evaluating an increase in portfolio allocations to private capital funds?

Alex Kokolis: I would tell investors and financial advisors to approach this shift with careful consideration and thorough due diligence. It is essential to understand the unique characteristics and risks associated with private capital funds by conducting comprehensive research, evaluating fund managers' track records, and assessing the alignment of these investments with investor objectives.

Additionally, advisors should communicate transparently with their clients, educating them about the benefits and potential drawbacks of alternative assets. By doing so, advisors can ensure that their clients are well-informed and comfortable with the investment decisions being made.

Ultimately, the goal is to create diversified and personalized portfolios that may enhance investors' financial well-being and address their concerns about volatility and enhanced diversification.

data.title: Trinity Capital: Solid Q1 Earnings Warrants Upgrade
data.description:
Overview

These are volatile times for Business Development Companies as the extended period of elevated interest rates has caused many of these assets to deteriorate in value. Trinity Capital (TRIN) is one of the BDCs that felt the increased pressure on earnings and the overall appeal has suffered as a result. I previously covered TRIN and issued a hold rating due to the premium valuation and inconsistent earnings. TRIN recently reported their Q1 earnings to kick off 2025, so I wanted to revisit this BDC to assess how conditions may have improved.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, TRIN's price has decreased by about 4.8%, with most of that decline taking place over the last few weeks in response to President Trump's tariff announcements. When including all distributions paid out during the same time frame, the total return jumps up to approximately 7.3%. Following the price decline, TRIN now offers a starting dividend yield of about 14.7%. This double-digit yield makes TRIN an enticing choice for investors trying to take advantage of this opportunity to lock in a higher yield. However, this assumes that TRIN can maintain the current distribution rate going forward.

Despite the rocky performance over recent years, there are still some qualities to like about TRIN. For instance, the portfolio remains diverse and is supported by a very low rate of non-accruals. Additionally, there were some fundamental improvements in the level of income generated from its investments. A lot of the outlook seems to depend on what the interest rate environment will look like going forward. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy and investment methodology that TRIN implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

As of the latest portfolio breakdown, TRIN has total assets amounting to $1.8B that is spread across a diverse range of industries. Diversity is a big part of TRIN's strategy because it mitigates any sort of concentration risk to any one specific industry. As we can see, Finance and Insurance investments make up the largest focus on the portfolio at 17.6% of assets. This is followed by exposure to investments in Medical Devices and SaaS (software-as-a-service) companies, accounting for 12.7% and 10.4% of assets respectively.

Additionally, TRIN puts a primary emphasis on floating rate investments, which currently makes up 76.9% of assets. This allows TRIN to capitalize on the higher interest rate environment; A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher interest income that can be collected from borrowers. However, higher rates are only beneficial if management's underwriting capabilities lead to precise investments into borrowers that have consistent cash flows and can navigate the higher cost of debt.

Unlike traditional BDCs, TRIN has two additional operating segments outside of its loan portfolio. This includes exposure to its equipment financing and equity & warrant segments. Since these other two segments make up a smaller percentage of TRIN's total operating income, they present very little risk while simultaneously expanding on its diversity. The breakdown is as follows:

  1. Loan Portfolio: $1.3B fair value spread across 81 different portfolio companies.
  2. Equipment Financings: $336.7M spread across 27 different portfolio companies.
  3. Equity & Warrants: $125.6M spread across 132 different portfolio companies.

The great thing about the addition of these other segments of the portfolio is that they can serve as a layer of defense against unfavorable interest rate changes. Looking at the chart below, we can see that there is estimated to be limited downside risk if interest rates were to get cut by 200-basis-points. A lower interest rate environment would likely result in lower net investment income in the loan portfolio of the portfolio, but may not have the same downside effect on the equity portion.

Lastly, the loan portion of their portfolio is structured as senior secured debt. Senior secured loans sit at the top of the corporate capital structure and have the highest priority for repayment. This helps minimize losses felt when a borrower underperforms as it reduces the risk that TRIN loses all invested capital. If a borrower is materially underperforming and defaults on their loan, they may need to liquidate assets. In this scenario, TRIN would be entitled for repayment first before other forms of debt.

Updated Valuation

Something that I've noticed about the valuation of BDCs is that the high-quality assets tend to consistently trade at a premium valuation. Conversely, the lower quality BDCs tend to trade at a discount to NAV. TRIN has remained somewhere in the between the two as the price has continuously bounced around and now trades at a slight premium to NAV of about 6%. For reference, TRIN has traded at an average premium to NAV closer to 5%, so the recent price decline has aligned TRIN's valuation closer to its average.

Wall Street analysts have an average price target of $16.07 per share, indicating a potential upside of 13.8% from the current level. While I do believe this sort of price recovery upwards is certainly possible, I think that the likeliness of it depends on the current interest rate environment. The Fed has decided to keep interest rates unchanged for their third meeting now, citing that they continue to measure the risks of higher unemployment and inflationary data. I think that two scenarios can play out here depending on whether or not rate cuts happen.

In the first scenario, let's assume that interest rates do get cut. As established, this would have a minimal impact on net investment income. Therefore, I see very little downside risk there. Lower rates would essentially create a more favorable borrowing environment, so I anticipate this causing an influx of new growth opportunities that TRIN could capitalize on. This scenario would also provide relief for existing borrowers that have loans on a floating rate basis, which could lead to higher valuations for TRIN's investments.

The second scenario is that interest rates are not cut, and we remain in an elevated environment. Assuming that TRIN's portfolio remains resilient, we can anticipate the continuation of high net investment income but slower NAV growth. A higher interest rate makes it more difficult for borrowers to grow in valuation since a larger portion of their earnings need to go towards debt maintenance. Higher rates could also start to deteriorate the portfolio and cause an increase in non-accruals.

Therefore, the current conditions can be a bit tricky. However, I believe that TRIN has demonstrated some improvements that could indicate positive momentum. For instance, TRIN's NAV has increased year over year. An increasing NAV is a sign that the underlying strategy is effective and the BDC has been able to consistently out earn its distribution. For reference, here are the quarter end NAV amounts:

  • Q1 2024: $12.88 per share
  • Q2 2024: $13.12 per share
  • Q3 2024: $13.13 per share
  • Q4 2024: $13.35 per share
  • Q1 2025: $13.05 per share

Q1 Earnings & Risk Profile

TRIN recently reported its Q1 earnings and I believe that conditions have improved and sets a good tone for the rest of the year. Most notably, net investment income landed at $0.52 per share, beating expectations by $0.13 per share. This was a slight decrease from the prior year's net investment income of $0.54 per share, but the results were still on par with the range over the last year. Total investment income amounted to $65.3M, which was a year-over-year increase from the prior amount of $50.4M.

Operating expenses also saw an increase up to $33M, from the prior year's total of $25.2M. Despite TRIN experiencing a small net realized loss of $2.1M during the quarter, the net increase in net assets from operations was substantially better than Q1 of 2024. The net increase in assets was $27M, compared to the prior year's $14.5M. As expected, 71.4% of the investment income came from the loan portion of the portfolio.

Additionally, management has remained committed to allocating capital towards new investments that can drive growth. TRIN was able to commit $185.9M towards new debt and equity investments. They increased the size of the portfolio by allocating a portion of this capital towards new brand-new portfolio companies. TRIN also has $8.3M in cash and equivalents available, so I am confident that the NAV growth can continue.

Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. Therefore, I like to keep track on how the rate of non-accruals shift over time because it can help gauge the overall quality of the portfolio, as well as management's underwriting abilities. Non-accruals can reveal sector-specific weaknesses or vulnerabilities to the interest rate environment. When I previously covered TRIN, non-accruals amounted to 0.8% of fair portfolio value.

As of the latest earnings call, the rate of non-accruals has slightly increased to 0.9% of fair portfolio value. Since the rate of non-accruals remains under 1%, I don't consider this marginal uptick as something to worry about. This can be attributed to one portfolio company being put into non-accrual status over the last quarter, with another being removed out from non-accrual status.

Quarter over quarter, the number of portfolio companies on non-accrual remained consistent at five. During Q1, one additional portfolio was added to the non-accrual list, while one existing non-accrual was realized in the quarter. Subsequent to quarter end, an additional non-accrual credit was realized at the Q1 fair value. - Gerry Harder, COO

Although non-accruals don't paint the entire picture, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs. We can see that TRIN has remained fairly competitive in this regard. TRIN has actually outperformed this peer group in total return over the last three years.

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Golub Capital (GBDC): 0.7% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Bain Capital Specialty Finance (BCSF): 0.2% non-accrual rate at fair value.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.51 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.7%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.52 per share for the quarter. This indicates that earnings currently support the distribution at a very tight coverage rate of approximately 102%. Just for reference, this is a significant decline in coverage from last quarter, when earnings supported the distribution at a rate of 113.7%. Since the coverage isn't as strong as it once was, I wouldn't expect any additional supplemental payments at the moment.

Although TRIN has been able to support distributions so far, I don't think it's likely that we experience a reduction in payouts. TRIN does have some spillover income of $1.04 per share that can be distributed, but management seems focused on prioritizing NAV growth from this point forward to drive better value for shareholders. While they may be able to increase the NAV without a distribution cut, a slight reduction would certainly be aligned with management's goal.

So increasing the dividend doesn't make a lot of sense because we're not getting a lot of credit for that. So building NAV and focusing on building NAV has been what we've done now for consecutive quarters. We'll probably continue doing that, but the Board will meet quarterly to decide whether or not we want to build that spillover. - Kyle Brown, CEO

It's worth noting that the distributions received from TRIN are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividend income has the least favorable tax consequences compared to the qualified dividends you may receive from more traditional dividend stocks. Based on your specific tax bracket, amount of capital invested, and specific income needs, TRIN may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset any tax implications.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on TRIN to a buy here. The price has fallen to a more favorable valuation and fundamentals have demonstrated some improvements in key areas. For instance, non-accruals remain very low and are competitive to peer BDCs. The earnings remain in a consistent range and management continues to allocate capital towards new investment activity to grow the net asset value. The distribution coverage may be a bit weaker, but new portfolio companies can help improve net investment income levels in the future. Additionally, TRIN has a lot of undistributed spillover income that can be tapped into to prevent a distribution cut. Lower interest rates can serve as a positive catalyst for growth going forward as well.

data.title: Clearwater Paper: Boring Papermill/Cardboard Maker, Potential 25% FCF Yield Over Time
data.description:
Clearwater Paper Corporation (NYSE:CLW) is a U.S. paper mill concern I have mentioned several times over the last five years as a buy idea. The stock is looking interesting again, after a major asset shuffle last year has cratered the share quote (at least initially).

The good news is future forecasted results may be very exciting in the next economic upturn, with management having a stated goal of $100 million in free cash flow generation per annum, over the coming economic cycle (typically lasting 5–7 years). If this can be pulled off, today's equity market capitalization of $444 million and enterprise value of $661 million are both entirely too cheap.

With price to sales and book value readings already reaching for recession-like lows, my view is strong outperformance of this name vs. the S&P 500 could become reality in the second half of 2025 and a number of years thereafter. Buying shares in the $25 to $30 range could be well worth the risk of holding through a potential U.S. economic contraction over the short term. Let me explain why.

Business Changes

Essentially, the asset shuffle was this: Clearwater bought the Augusta Paperboard Mill in May for $700 million and sold its tissue business for $1.06 billion in November 2024.

The two transactions have allowed operations to be more targeted and focused, while the balance sheet has eliminated most of its debt. The only major remaining long-term debt issue of $275 million is due in 2028 (at 4.75% for an ultra-low interest expense), meaning little in the way of refinancing or interest cost will be reality for another three years.

Today, Clearwater operates 3 paperboard manufacturing/milling facilities and 4 sheeting plants in America, producing cups and boxes for individual customer needs as its core competency.

Over the years, I have personally invested in many refocused businesses with cleaned-up balance sheets that performed amazingly well over the next year or two. So, the new Clearwater operating setup has captured my attention in early 2025.

Management's clearly stated operating goals are listed in the slide below from its May 2025 Investor Presentation. With the business model now focused on one industry holding relatively stable economics, further operating cost reductions on top of debt repayments from new cash flow generation, could in combination support $100+ million a year in FCF soon. In addition, Clearwater managers have committed themselves to returning shareholder capital through buybacks and dividends over time.

Another positive, the company is striving to be a leader in sustainability, making products that can/should be recycled, from renewable tree resources, with limited pollution of the environment from its plants, using clean energy where possible to power its buildings and machinery.

Attractive Valuation on Future Results

The other desirable part of the investment story for Clearwater is its valuation appears to be discounting a rotten future for the company. I strongly disagree with this assessment. Today's valuation is a function of the company's metamorphosis into a much better version of itself by the end of 2025, during a slowing economic backdrop.

All told, I can argue the company is trading at its lowest long-term valuation ever, since going public in late 2008. If you are hunting for a beat-up, contrarian buy with plenty of things going for it, Clearwater should be near the top of your research list.

On either price or net enterprise value to trailing sales (2025 revenues are projected to be similar to 2024 on the asset switches, using continuing operation data), the 0.26x and 0.38x ratios respectively are similar or lower than the depths of the 2020 COVID pandemic or 2008-09 Great Recession.

Just as appealing, the price to tangible book value number of 0.56x is about the cheapest it has ever been (outside of a few months during the Great Recession). In contrast, this statistic has almost always been above 1.0x, with a 17-year median annual average closer to 2.5x.

The market seems to have forgotten most of Clearwater's debt has been paid off. The enterprise value of $661 million at $27 per share in May 2025 is the biggest discount to tangible book value EVER. An average valuation premium for EV to tangible BV of $700 million would be the same as a stock quote closer to $75 per share.

After this year's integration and rationalization process is complete, earnings are projected by Wall Street analysts to jump to $2.93 next year. Such would translate into a forward P/E ratio around 9x.

How does a forward estimated P/E of 9x compare to packaging and paperboard peers/competitors? Quite well is the answer. Clearwater has fallen from the most expensive to one of the cheapest picks over the past year of trading on projected earnings power.

But that's not the only variable to contemplate on the earnings front. We have to properly weigh Clearwater is now operating with very little leverage/debt on its balance sheet in May. The debt-to-equity reading is now the lowest (strongest for fiscal conservatism) reading of the group.

Interest expense estimated at $15 million annually on $1.59 billion in projected 2025 sales is equally a low percentage out of the group. This number represents just 0.9% of sales. In comparison, International Paper (IP) is sporting an equivalent level of interest expense on sales of 2.3%, while Amcor PLC (AMCR) is sitting at 2.5%. All other variables being exactly the same, Clearwater shareholders would be able to keep this extra percentage in their pocket, as opposed to sending it to banks and bondholders.

Generally, slighter leverage ratios translate into higher valuations on earnings for an equity, not lower. I find a complete disconnect with this idea currently.

Bottoming Chart Pattern

The last important piece of my investment puzzle is to review the trading chart. In many respects, intense selling pressure has been fading since December.

The Negative Volume Index (using low-volume days as an indicator) has been rising for five months now (bottom circled in green below). On Balance, Volume reversed all the way back in September (circled in blue). Together, both have been flashing a bullish non-confirmation signal of the price low in March.

Then, the 20-day Chaikin Money Flow indicator flipped into a positive range in early April (boxed in gold). All told, the whole technical picture is dramatically more constructive than in September. Underlying momentum trends haven't looked this positive since last May.

Final Thoughts

In my view, an extra-credit bullish variable for investors is that 90% of total 2024 sales were made inside America (and the revamped asset setup will not change this number much), meaning little is exported overseas. Measured against larger conglomerates in the paper mill world, CLW should be negatively affected less by any retaliatory tariffs instigated by foreign governments in President Trump's trade war.

Honestly, U.S.-focused business operations are another great reason to consider Clearwater Paper for your portfolio. I suspect a better overall competitive advantage (relative product pricing power) could be approaching if Canadian tariff rates rise further on goods imported here.

I would note as a risk, basic wood input costs could rise marginally, depending on the market effects of tariffs on Canadian pulp imported into America. But, any dramatic change in raw material costs will also affect competitors operating inside the U.S.

I know it's hard to pull the trigger and buy a stock, when the price has come down, a business is restructuring, and a transition year financially provides little daylight/perspective about the future. Seeking Alpha's Quant and Factor ratings outline a less than satisfactory investment background in May 2025.

You have to remember, investors need to be forward looking in this situation. The clean balance sheet is worth owning. You are buying net hard assets at a price well below replacement costs (tangible book value). Plus, 2025 could be a U.S. recession year, where sales and margins are weaker than normal.

I would not be surprised by one final downdraft in price closer to $20 this year, before a rebound back towards tangible book value plays out into 2026. Consequently, I own a small position, standing prepared to increase my stake on weakness.

For sure, the compounding math of a $40 to $50 price target ($48 tangible book value) is much better with an entry in the low-$20s vs. closer to $30. Yet, I cannot guarantee another price slide is coming.

The investment summary is Clearwater Paper's valuation story and chart pattern have captured my attention at today's $27 quote. Waiting may mean you miss out on a terrific entry opportunity. I rate shares a Buy under $30, with Strong Buy territory pegged under $23 (near the 52-week low in March).
